meta.ariaLabel: Tree of the historical case for Jesus' resurrection

aboutModal.title: About this tree
aboutModal.bodyHtml: This tree organizes the case around three major historical branches: the empty tomb, the appearances, and the origin of resurrection belief. It uses William Lane Craig's evidential structure as a backbone, but also incorporates Paul's undisputed letters, selected historical arguments associated with N. T. Wright, and limited external corroboration where it sharpens the case.

name: Resurrection Case
subtitle: Empty tomb, appearances, and risen belief

children.name: Empty Tomb
children.subtitle: Jesus' tomb was found empty

children.name: Burial
children.subtitle: The burial both supports and implies the empty tomb

children.name: Implies Empty Tomb
children.subtitle: Why a historical burial points toward an empty grave

children.name: Belief Conflict
children.subtitle: Resurrection belief conflicts with an occupied tomb

children.name: Jewish Meaning
children.subtitle: Resurrection did not mean a body left in the grave
children.details: In first-century Jewish usage, resurrection referred to the dead being raised, not merely exalted, remembered, or spiritually vindicated while the body remained in the tomb. If Jesus was known to be buried in a specific grave, proclaiming resurrection would naturally conflict with an occupied tomb.

source.title: Craig on burial and resurrection meaning

children.name: Jerusalem Test
children.subtitle: An occupied tomb would have crippled the message in Jerusalem

children.name: Place of Crucifixion
children.subtitle: The claim arose where the death and burial occurred
children.details: The resurrection message did not begin in a distant city insulated from local knowledge. It was proclaimed in Jerusalem, the place where Jesus had been crucified and buried, so an occupied known tomb would have directly undercut persuasion there.

source.title: Craig on public proclamation in Jerusalem

children.name: Hostile Reply
children.subtitle: Opponents had motive and opportunity to contest the claim

children.name: Authorities Motivated
children.subtitle: Opposition was real, not indifferent
children.details: The suggestion that Jewish authorities would not have cared enough to challenge the Christian claim sits poorly with the evidence of intense early opposition. Paul himself says he violently persecuted the church, showing that the movement was treated as dangerous enough to suppress.

children.name: Occupied Tomb
children.subtitle: A known body would have been the obvious rebuttal
children.details: If Jesus had remained in a known tomb, the simplest counter to resurrection preaching would have been to point to the occupied grave. Craig makes this public-checkability point explicitly in arguing from burial to empty tomb.

source.title: Craig on the occupied tomb rebuttal

children.name: No Body Dispute
children.subtitle: We have no evidence of a remains-identification controversy
children.details: If the body had still been available but only poorly identifiable, one would expect some trace of a dispute over remains, location, or identity. The surviving early evidence contains no such rival controversy. This is an inference from the shape of the evidence rather than a direct quotation from Craig.

source.title: Early resurrection tradition and public opposition

children.name: Burial Evidence
children.subtitle: Why the burial itself is historically credible

children.name: Early Core
children.subtitle: The burial appears in very early tradition

children.name: 1 Cor 15
children.subtitle: Paul passes on burial tradition he had already received
children.details: Paul says he delivered what he had also received, and the formula includes Jesus' burial. Because Paul is recounting what he had already passed on, the tradition predates 1 Corinthians itself.

children.name: Very Early
children.subtitle: Many scholars date the tradition to within a few years
children.details: The pre-Pauline tradition in 1 Corinthians 15 is commonly dated far earlier than the letter, often within a few years of the crucifixion. Habermas is a widely cited representative of that judgment, and Wright likewise says the material is considerably older than 1 Corinthians itself.

source.title: Habermas and Wright on the early creed

children.name: Outline and Expansion
children.subtitle: The early formula is fleshed out in narrative and preaching

children.name: Creedal Outline
children.subtitle: Buried, raised, appeared
children.details: The formula in 1 Corinthians 15 gives a compact outline: death, burial, resurrection, appearances. It is brief, but it establishes the burial as part of the earliest proclamation.

children.name: Acts 13
children.subtitle: Acts preserves the burial sequence in preaching form
children.details: Acts 13:28-31 gives the sequence of death, taking down, burial, and resurrection in summary sermon form. Craig treats Acts as preserving early apostolic preaching substance, even if Luke shapes the wording.

children.name: Mark 15-16
children.subtitle: Mark preserves the fleshed-out burial narrative
children.details: Mark 15:37-16:7 gives the concrete burial and empty-tomb narrative. Craig treats this material as rooted in pre-Markan passion tradition rather than as late literary invention.

children.name: Source Lines
children.subtitle: The burial is attested across multiple streams

children.name: Mark
children.subtitle: Joseph buries Jesus in a tomb
children.details: Mark gives the core burial account featuring Joseph of Arimathea, the tomb, the stone, and the women observing the site.

children.name: Matthew
children.subtitle: Matthew provides additional attestation
children.details: Matthew includes the burial with distinctive material of his own, adding further attestation rather than merely reproducing Mark without development.

children.name: Luke
children.subtitle: Luke also attests the burial with his own shaping
children.details: Luke includes the burial account with substantive wording and emphasis differences, contributing another witness-line to the burial tradition.

children.name: John
children.subtitle: John likely preserves an independent burial line
children.details: Craig treats John as an additional likely independent source at this point, which would add another witness-line for the burial tradition.

source.title: Craig on John as an independent source

children.name: Acts
children.subtitle: Acts may preserve early preaching summaries
children.details: Craig says Luke's speeches in Acts are not likely fabricated out of whole cloth but preserve the substance of apostolic preaching. That gives Acts evidential value for the burial proclamation.

source.title: Craig on Acts speeches preserving early preaching

children.name: Joseph
children.subtitle: Joseph of Arimathea is a strong historical marker

children.name: Sanhedrin Member
children.subtitle: An awkward figure to invent
children.details: Joseph is identified as a member of the very council associated with Jesus' condemnation, which makes him a surprising choice for a fabricated Christian hero.

children.name: Named Person
children.subtitle: The burier is specific, not anonymous
children.details: The tradition identifies Joseph by name and role, which increases the concreteness of the report.

children.name: Observed Site
children.subtitle: The tomb location was observed and remembered

children.name: Mark
children.subtitle: The women saw where he was laid
children.details: Mark explicitly says Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses saw where Jesus was laid.

children.name: Luke
children.subtitle: Luke also says the women saw the tomb and body placement
children.details: Luke says the women followed, saw the tomb, and saw how his body was laid.

children.name: Narrative Link
children.subtitle: The observed site explains the return visit
children.details: Because the women observed the burial location, the empty-tomb visit functions as a return to a known tomb rather than a confused search.

source.title: Craig on burial and empty-tomb linkage

children.name: Early Evidence
children.subtitle: The empty-tomb claim is early

children.name: Pre-Markan Tomb
children.subtitle: The empty-tomb story is very old
children.details: Craig argues that the empty-tomb story, like the burial story, is part of the pre-Markan passion tradition and is therefore very early rather than a late appendix.

source.title: Craig on the pre-Markan empty-tomb story

children.name: Third Day
children.subtitle: The dating likely reflects discovery day
children.details: Craig argues that the phrase 'on the third day' probably points back to the day the empty tomb was discovered, since no one was said to have witnessed the resurrection itself. That makes the formula in 1 Corinthians 15 indirect early evidence for the empty tomb.

children.name: No Rival Burial
children.subtitle: No competing burial tradition is preserved
children.details: Craig lists the absence of competing burial traditions as one factor supporting the historical reliability of the burial account. That strengthens the move from known burial to empty tomb.

source.title: Craig on the absence of competing burial traditions

children.name: Women Witnesses
children.subtitle: The discoverers are unlikely inventions

children.name: Low Status
children.subtitle: Women were not ideal apologetic witnesses
children.details: Craig treats the women as the primary discoverers of the empty tomb as a mark of historical authenticity, since women were not the most advantageous public witnesses to invent for apologetic effect in that setting.

children.name: Narrative Fit
children.subtitle: Their role arises naturally from the burial story
children.details: The same women who see where Jesus is laid are the ones who return to the tomb. Their presence is coherent within the story rather than artificially inserted at the point of discovery.

source.title: Craig on burial and empty-tomb linkage

children.name: Public Challenge
children.subtitle: The claim emerged where it could be checked

children.name: Jerusalem Setting
children.subtitle: The message arose where the tomb mattered
children.details: The resurrection proclamation arose in the same broad setting where Jesus had been executed and buried. That gives the empty-tomb claim public and historical weight rather than the feel of a distant legend.

source.title: Craig on the known grave and public challenge

children.name: Jewish Reply
children.subtitle: Hostile response still assumes a missing body
children.details: Craig argues that the Jewish counterstory presupposes the empty tomb: it attempts to explain why the body was not there rather than denying the issue existed.

children.name: Appearances
children.subtitle: Jesus appeared alive after death

children.name: Early Creed
children.subtitle: Paul transmits received witness tradition

children.name: Received Tradition
children.subtitle: Paul passes on what he received
children.details: Paul says he delivered to the Corinthians what he had also received as of first importance. The witness-list therefore belongs to tradition earlier than the letter itself.

children.name: Named Transmitter
children.subtitle: The tradition comes through Paul himself
children.details: The appearance tradition is not anonymously preserved. It is transmitted by Paul, a named figure whose own letters survive and who places himself within the witness-list.

children.name: Known Pillars
children.subtitle: Paul knew Cephas, James, and John
children.details: Paul says he spent fifteen days with Cephas, saw James the Lord's brother, and later identifies James, Cephas, and John as pillars. That means the tradition is being transmitted by someone personally connected to central leaders of the earliest church.

children.name: Named Witnesses
children.subtitle: Specific individuals are identified

children.name: Peter
children.subtitle: Cephas is named in the witness-list
children.details: The witness-list names Cephas, Peter's Aramaic name. That supports the primitive character of the tradition and anchors it to a specific person.

children.name: James
children.subtitle: James is named as a witness
children.details: The witness-list singles out James, and Paul independently knows James personally. This matters because James later stands as a prominent Jerusalem leader rather than an obscure figure in the tradition.

children.name: Paul
children.subtitle: Paul includes himself as final witness
children.details: Paul adds himself at the end of the witness-list. He also reminds readers elsewhere that he had formerly persecuted the church, which gives special historical weight to his testimony.

children.name: Group Witness
children.subtitle: The appearances were not merely private

children.name: The Twelve
children.subtitle: A group appearance belongs to the earliest formula
children.details: The witness-list includes an appearance to the Twelve, showing that the evidence is not limited to one private encounter.

children.name: Five Hundred
children.subtitle: Paul names a large body of witnesses
children.details: Paul says Jesus appeared to more than five hundred at one time, most of whom were still alive though some had fallen asleep. That makes the claim look publicly exposed rather than insulated. Craig, following C. H. Dodd, treats Paul's comment as implying that these witnesses were in principle available to be questioned.

children.name: All the Apostles
children.subtitle: The witness-list extends beyond the Twelve
children.details: After James, Paul adds 'all the apostles,' showing that the witness tradition extends beyond Peter, James, and the Twelve alone.

children.name: Hostile Proximity
children.subtitle: The transmitter came from early opposition

children.name: Former Persecutor
children.subtitle: Paul attacked the church before joining it
children.details: Paul says he intensely persecuted the church of God and tried to destroy it. The appearance tradition therefore comes to us through someone who entered the movement from early hostile proximity rather than from late, detached sympathy.

children.name: No Rival Core
children.subtitle: No early counter-tradition appears in Paul's letters
children.details: This is an inference from the evidence structure: Paul was both an early opponent and later a close associate of Cephas and James, yet his letters preserve no rival primitive formula in which Jesus remained in an occupied tomb while followers merely had comforting visions. The earliest form available to us is already resurrection-centered.

children.name: Risen Belief
children.subtitle: The resurrection belief arose early and specifically

children.name: Early Belief
children.subtitle: The resurrection message is present from the start

children.name: From the Start
children.subtitle: The earliest surviving formula is resurrection-centered
children.details: The earliest explicit summary we have is already specific: Christ died, was buried, was raised, and appeared. Resurrection is not a late doctrinal embellishment added after a period of vaguer belief.

children.name: Jerusalem Root
children.subtitle: The belief emerged close to the events
children.details: Paul's contact with Cephas, James, and John places the transmitted belief in direct relation to the Jerusalem leadership. The belief is not traceable first in a distant or anonymous environment.

children.name: Specific Claim
children.subtitle: The claim was resurrection, not mere spiritual survival

children.name: Bodily Meaning
children.subtitle: Resurrection had concrete content
children.details: Craig argues that, in first-century Jewish thought, 'he was buried' followed by 'he was raised' carried bodily implications. Wright likewise stresses that early Christians retained the Jewish concept of resurrection but made it sharper and more precise around Jesus himself.

source.title: Craig and Wright on resurrection meaning

children.name: No Vision-Only Core
children.subtitle: The earliest pattern is not merely visionary
children.details: The sources do not preserve a primitive formula saying merely that Jesus lived on spiritually or was exalted in vision language alone. The earliest recoverable core already centers on resurrection and appearances.

children.name: Hard Sell
children.subtitle: The claim did not fit ordinary expectations

children.name: Jewish Surprise
children.subtitle: One man raised in advance was not the expected pattern
children.details: Wright argues that early Christians retained Jewish resurrection belief but reshaped it around Jesus in a way demanding explanation. Instead of choosing a new messiah after Jesus' death, they continued to proclaim Jesus himself as Messiah because they believed he had been raised.

source.title: N. T. Wright on the rise of Christian resurrection belief

children.name: Greek Resistance
children.subtitle: The message was foolishness to Gentiles
children.details: Paul says the Christian proclamation was a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles. That makes the resurrection message a hard sell across both cultural worlds rather than an easy invention designed for immediate plausibility.

children.name: No New Messiah
children.subtitle: They did not simply replace Jesus
children.details: Wright notes that when messianic movements lost their leader, the normal task was to find another claimant. The early Christians did not do that; they continued to speak of Jesus himself as Messiah. That is one of the historical features demanding explanation.

source.title: N. T. Wright on early Christian messiah belief

children.name: Changed Lives
children.subtitle: The belief is reflected in major reversals

children.name: James Changed
children.subtitle: From unbelief to central leadership
children.details: James is named in the witness-list and later appears as a central Jerusalem leader. Josephus also independently attests James as 'the brother of Jesus, who was called Christ,' showing that James is a historical figure anchored outside the New Testament as well.

source.title: Josephus on James, brother of Jesus called Christ

children.name: Paul Changed
children.subtitle: From persecutor to proclaimer
children.details: Paul's own letters present a sharp reversal: the man who formerly persecuted the church is now preaching the faith he once tried to destroy. That gives special force to the rise of resurrection belief.

children.name: Movement Endures
children.subtitle: The slain leader still had followers
children.details: Josephus' reference to James as the brother of Jesus who was called Christ shows that Jesus still had an identifiable movement later in the first century. Combined with Paul's undisputed letters, the documented message attached to that movement is resurrection, not mere admiration for a dead teacher.

source.title: Josephus on James and Jesus called Christ

children.name: External Anchors
children.subtitle: Some key figures and setting are externally confirmed

children.name: James in Josephus
children.subtitle: James is independently attested
children.details: Josephus records the execution of James and identifies him as the brother of Jesus who was called Christ. That externally anchors a major figure in the resurrection tradition.

source.title: Josephus on James, brother of Jesus called Christ

children.name: John the Baptist
children.subtitle: The New Testament setting has external support
children.details: Josephus also attests John the Baptist, confirming that the wider movement-world of the Gospels includes figures known outside Christian sources.

children.name: Pilate and Christus
children.subtitle: Jesus' execution and movement are externally noted
children.details: Tacitus reports that Christus was executed by Pontius Pilate and that the movement persisted afterward. This does not prove the resurrection, but it externally confirms the execution context and continued Christian movement.
